title: Social Class and Educational Achievement
examTip: Organise your factors as 'internal' (inside school) and 'external' (outside school) — AQA mark schemes are structured this way and examiners reward this distinction.
introTips: Know Bourdieu's concept of cultural capital (1984) — it's the most cited explanation in this topic.
Bernstein's language codes (1975) — restricted vs elaborated — is a high-value concept many students miss.
The self-fulfilling prophecy (Rosenthal and Jacobson, 1968) links internal and external factors perfectly.
intro: Here is one of the most uncomfortable facts in British education: where you grow up and how much money your parents earn predicts your exam results more reliably than your raw ability. Sociologists call this the class achievement gap, and it's been documented for decades. Understanding why it happens — and what (if anything) can be done about it — is one of sociology's most important and contested questions.

keyPoints.heading: Evidence for class differences in achievement
keyPoints.content: The relationship between social class and educational achievement is one of the most robust findings in British sociology. At GCSE: approximately 75% of pupils from the highest socioeconomic groups achieve grades 9–4 in English and maths, compared to around 40% from the lowest (Sutton Trust). At university: 57% of young people from higher education-educated families go to university, compared with 25% from non-HE families. Elite universities: Oxford and Cambridge take over 60% of their UK students from fee-paying schools, which educate only 7% of the pupil population — a staggering overrepresentation. Pupils eligible for free school meals (FSM) — used as a proxy for poverty — consistently achieve 30–40 percentage points below non-FSM pupils nationally. These gaps appear at every stage of education, from EYFS baseline assessments through to graduate employment.
writeBullets: GCSE: ~75% of highest socioeconomic group achieves 9-4 vs ~40% from lowest (Sutton Trust)
University: 57% of HE-educated families' children go to university vs 25% from non-HE families
Oxford and Cambridge: 60%+ from fee-paying schools (which educate only 7% of pupils)
Free school meals (FSM): persistent 30–40 percentage point gap vs non-FSM nationally
Class gap appears at every stage — from early years through to graduate employment
FSM eligibility used as a proxy for poverty and deprivation in education research
keyPoints.memTip: The '7% rule': private schools educate 7% of pupils but provide 60%+ of Oxbridge students. That's the class gap in one number.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Opening any answer with this stark statistic immediately signals confident use of evidence to the examiner.

keyPoints.heading: External factors: material deprivation
keyPoints.content: Material deprivation refers to the direct effects of poverty on educational achievement. Howard (2001) found that low family income affects diet, health, and cognitive development: poor nutrition → poor concentration, more illness, lower measured IQ. Housing: overcrowded homes mean no quiet space to study, more illness from poor conditions, sleep deprivation from noise, and stress from family financial pressures. J.W.B. Douglas (1964) — 'The Home and School' — found home conditions and parental attitudes were the single strongest predictor of educational achievement, even more so than school quality. Poverty also limits access to educational resources: books, computers, private tutors, educational trips, music lessons, enrichment activities. Financial pressure often means teenagers from low-income families take part-time jobs that compete with study time. Pre-school disadvantage: children from deprived backgrounds are less likely to have accessed high-quality early years education. Sugarman (1970) argued working-class culture adapts to material conditions — developing values of 'fatalism' (accepting circumstances as fixed) and 'immediate gratification' (prioritising present enjoyment over future planning) which disadvantage pupils in education.
writeBullets: Material deprivation: poverty directly affects diet, health, housing, and access to resources
Howard (2001): poor nutrition → poor concentration, illness, lower IQ
Douglas (1964): home conditions strongest predictor of achievement — home environment matters more than school quality
No quiet study space, no books/computers, no tutors — resource gap between classes
Part-time work competes with study time for older pupils in low-income families
Sugarman (1970): fatalism and immediate gratification as cultural adaptations to material poverty
keyPoints.memTip: Material deprivation = HARD = Housing, Access to resources, Resources (books/tech), Diet. All affected by poverty.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Douglas (1964) is a classic study students often forget — name-dropping it alongside Bourdieu shows strong breadth of knowledge.

keyPoints.heading: External factors: cultural deprivation and capital
keyPoints.content: Pierre Bourdieu (1984) introduced the concept of cultural capital: the cultural tastes, knowledge, skills and ways of speaking and thinking that are valued by schools and educational institutions. Middle-class culture is much closer to school culture — middle-class pupils already know how to talk to teachers, understand what is expected of them, and feel they belong in educational environments. Working-class pupils face a cultural capital deficit — their home culture is valid, but it doesn't match what schools reward. Basil Bernstein (1975) identified two language codes: the restricted code — context-dependent, limited vocabulary, informal, assumes shared knowledge (characteristic of working-class speech) — and the elaborated code — formal, context-independent, complex grammar, explicit explanations (the language of school, exams, and the middle class). Pupils whose home language is the restricted code must switch between two codes at school — a significant disadvantage. Douglas (1964) also found that middle-class parents were more likely to visit school, show interest in their children's progress, and convey the 'value' of education — these parental attitudes had a measurable impact on outcomes.
writeBullets: Bourdieu (1984): cultural capital — tastes, knowledge, ways of speaking valued by school
Middle-class culture matches school culture — working-class culture doesn't, creating a deficit
Bernstein (1975): restricted code (informal, context-dependent) vs elaborated code (formal, explicit)
School operates in elaborated code — working-class pupils disadvantaged from the start
Douglas (1964): middle-class parents visit school more, show greater interest — higher pupil achievement
Parental attitudes to education transmit the 'value' of schooling to children
keyPoints.memTip: Bernstein: Restricted code = short, informal, shared context. Elaborated code = long, formal, explained. Schools use Elaborated code — E for Exam language.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Bourdieu's cultural capital is the most widely known concept in this topic — make sure to explain it clearly and then apply it to education.

keyPoints.heading: Internal factors: labelling and streaming
keyPoints.content: Howard Becker (1971) interviewed 60 Chicago high school teachers and found they judged pupils against a mental image of the 'ideal pupil' — well-dressed, well-spoken, attentive, motivated — essentially a middle-class image. Working-class and Black Caribbean pupils were more likely to be labelled negatively. Labels can become a master status — the label 'troublemaker' or 'not academic' overrides all other characteristics. Rosenthal and Jacobson (1968) — 'Pygmalion in the Classroom' — demonstrated the self-fulfilling prophecy: teachers told certain pupils were 'intellectual bloomers' (randomly selected) subsequently showed significantly greater IQ gains a year later, because teachers unconsciously gave them more encouragement, harder work, and warmer feedback. Streaming (fixed ability groups for all subjects) tends to reproduce class divisions: working-class pupils are disproportionately placed in lower streams (Gillborn and Youdell, 2000). In lower streams: lower expectations, lower-tier exam entry, fewer experienced teachers, stigma, and the self-fulfilling prophecy of low expectations. Lacey (1970) — differentiation (teachers sorting pupils into high/low) leads to polarisation (pupils forming pro-school and anti-school subcultures in response).
writeBullets: Becker (1971): teachers have an 'ideal pupil' concept — middle-class image; working-class pupils labelled negatively
Master status: a label like 'troublemaker' overrides all other characteristics
Rosenthal and Jacobson (1968): self-fulfilling prophecy — teacher expectations shape pupil performance
Streaming reproduces class divisions — working-class pupils disproportionately in lower streams
Lower streams: lower expectations, lower-tier exams, fewer resources, stigma — cycle of underachievement
Lacey (1970): differentiation (labelling) → polarisation (anti-school vs pro-school subcultures)
keyPoints.memTip: Labelling chain: Becker labels → Rosenthal's prophecy fulfils itself → Lacey's polarisation into subcultures. Three steps, three names.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The self-fulfilling prophecy is one of the most important concepts across the whole sociology spec — it connects labelling in both education and crime.

keyPoints.heading: Policy responses to the class achievement gap
keyPoints.content: Governments have tried a range of policies to reduce the class achievement gap, with mixed results. Compensatory education: the idea that extra resources can compensate for disadvantage at home. Examples: Sure Start — New Labour's early intervention programme for 0–4 year olds in deprived areas (pre-school learning, health support, family advice); Educational Priority Areas (1960s) — extra funding for schools in deprived neighbourhoods. Pupil Premium: introduced 2011 — additional funding (approximately £1,000+ per pupil) for schools for each FSM-eligible or looked-after child; schools must publicly demonstrate how they spend it. Free School Meals: extended to all infant-aged pupils (Reception to Year 2) in 2014. Educational Maintenance Allowance (EMA): £30/week payment for 16–18 year olds from low-income families to stay in education — abolished in England 2011 but continues in Wales. Critical view: Bernstein (1970) argued 'you cannot compensate for society' — educational interventions cannot overcome deep structural economic inequality; poverty itself must be addressed. Policies are often short-term and underfunded relative to the scale of the problem.
writeBullets: Sure Start: early intervention 0–4 years in deprived areas — pre-school learning and family support
Educational Priority Areas (1960s): extra funding for schools in deprived neighbourhoods
Pupil Premium (2011): ~£1,000+ per FSM pupil; schools must demonstrate how it is spent
Free school meals: extended to all infants (Reception–Year 2) in 2014
EMA: £30/week for 16–18 year olds from low-income families — abolished England 2011
Bernstein (1970): 'you cannot compensate for society' — structural poverty must be tackled, not just education
keyPoints.memTip: SPEED = Sure Start, Pupil Premium, EMA, Educational Priority Areas, Dinner (free meals). Five compensatory policies.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Bernstein's critique of compensatory education is a powerful evaluative tool — use it to challenge the idea that education alone can close the gap.

tables.title: Internal vs external factors in class achievement
rows: FSM pupils achieve 30–40% below non-FSM; no quiet study space; poor nutrition affects concentration
Middle-class culture matches school culture; working-class pupils feel culturally out of place
Schools operate in elaborated code; restricted code speakers disadvantaged in exams and teacher interaction
Middle-class parents visit school more, transmit educational values — measurable impact on outcomes
Teachers judge against middle-class ideal; negative labels become master status
False 'bloomer' labels led to genuine IQ gains — teacher expectation shapes performance
Working-class pupils disproportionately in lower streams; lower-tier exams; stigma and low expectations

0.question: What does Bourdieu (1984) mean by 'cultural capital'?
options: The cultural tastes, knowledge and ways of speaking that schools value — which middle-class pupils have more of
The money spent on cultural activities like music lessons and museum visits
The ability to speak a foreign language
The economic wealth that wealthy families can invest in their children's education
0.correctFeedback: Correct! Cultural capital (Bourdieu, 1984) is the cultural tastes, knowledge, vocabulary, and ways of thinking and speaking that schools value — and which middle-class pupils acquire at home, giving them a massive hidden advantage.
0.wrongFeedback: Cultural capital (Bourdieu, 1984) is not about money — it's about cultural knowledge, tastes, ways of speaking and thinking that schools value. Middle-class pupils absorb this at home; working-class pupils face a 'cultural capital deficit'.

2.question: Bernstein (1975) argued that working-class pupils use a 'restricted code'. What is this?
options: Informal, context-dependent language that assumes shared knowledge — different from the elaborated code used in schools and exams
A simplified version of English taught in lower-ability sets
A secret language used by working-class pupils to exclude teachers
A government restriction on the vocabulary allowed in school textbooks
2.correctFeedback: Exactly right. The restricted code (Bernstein, 1975) is informal, context-dependent speech that assumes shared understanding — perfectly effective in everyday conversation, but different from the elaborated code schools and exams require.
2.wrongFeedback: The restricted code (Bernstein, 1975) is informal, context-dependent language that assumes shared background knowledge — it's not inferior, but it's different from the elaborated code (formal, explicit, context-independent) that schools and exam papers use.

3.question: What did Rosenthal and Jacobson (1968) demonstrate in their 'Pygmalion in the Classroom' experiment?
options: That teacher expectations shape pupil performance — pupils told they were 'bloomers' showed greater IQ gains
That streaming into ability groups improves outcomes for all pupils
That middle-class pupils naturally outperform working-class pupils in IQ tests
That cultural capital determines educational success from birth
3.correctFeedback: Correct! Rosenthal and Jacobson (1968) showed the self-fulfilling prophecy in action: pupils randomly labelled as 'intellectual bloomers' actually gained more IQ points over a year — because teacher expectations unconsciously changed how those pupils were treated.
3.wrongFeedback: Rosenthal and Jacobson (1968) demonstrated the self-fulfilling prophecy: randomly selected 'bloomer' pupils showed greater IQ gains because teacher expectations changed how they were taught and encouraged — showing expectations can create the reality they predict.

commonMistakes: Listing only external factors and forgetting internal ones (labelling, streaming, self-fulfilling prophecy) — always cover both categories.
Describing cultural capital as being about money — it is specifically about cultural knowledge, tastes, and ways of speaking, not economic wealth.
Saying Bernstein's restricted code is 'worse' — it is equally valid for everyday communication, just different from what schools reward; avoid value judgements.
Forgetting to evaluate policies — Bernstein's 'you cannot compensate for society' (1970) is the standard critical response to compensatory education.
Confusing streaming (all subjects) with setting (subject-by-subject) — AQA distinguishes between these, so be precise.
examTips: Structure your answer by type: 'External factors include material deprivation (Howard, 2001) and cultural capital (Bourdieu, 1984)… Internal factors include labelling (Becker, 1971) and the self-fulfilling prophecy (Rosenthal and Jacobson, 1968)…'
Always evaluate whether policies work — Sure Start was largely positive (evaluations showed improved outcomes) but was cut after 2010. Use this as evidence that political will matters.

To challenge meritocracy, combine two pieces of evidence: 'Sutton Trust data shows 7% of pupils in private schools supply 60%+ of Oxbridge students, while Bourdieu (1984) shows cultural capital, not ability, drives this.'
For extended writing, end with Bernstein's critique: education alone cannot overcome structural inequality — poverty must be addressed at its root.
revisionCardBullets: GCSE gap: ~75% (highest class) vs ~40% (lowest class) achieving 9-4 in English and maths (Sutton Trust)
7% rule: private schools → 60%+ of Oxbridge students
FSM pupils: 30–40% gap nationally compared to non-FSM
Material deprivation: poverty → poor diet, housing, health, no resources (Howard, 2001; Douglas, 1964)
Bourdieu (1984): cultural capital — middle-class culture matches school culture; working-class has deficit
Bernstein (1975): restricted code (home) vs elaborated code (school) — mismatch disadvantages working-class
Becker (1971): 'ideal pupil' concept — working-class pupils labelled negatively; master status
Rosenthal and Jacobson (1968): self-fulfilling prophecy — teacher expectations shape performance
Streaming reproduces class divisions (Lacey, 1970 — differentiation → polarisation)
Sure Start: early intervention 0–4 years in deprived areas
Bernstein (1970): 'you cannot compensate for society' — structural poverty must be tackled
